function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Viasat's most recent 10-K discloses satellite depreciation as a significant line item within its space segment, and Viasat has historically reported negative or near-zero segment operating income despite positive EBITDA, precisely because satellite useful-life assumptions drive a large non-cash depreciation charge. Using SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity segment operating income and Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025, calculate the implied depreciation and amortization add-back embedded in the EBITDA bridge. Compare this gap directionally to Viasat's disclosed satellite depreciation as a percentage of satellite-segment revenue. What does the comparison reveal about how LEO constellation economics — with higher satellite counts, shorter useful lives, and continuous replenishment — structurally differ from GEO satellite depreciation profiles, and why does this make EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion particularly important when evaluating LEO broadband operators as a class?
I'll tackle this systematically. Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and searching for Viasat's most recent 10-K simultaneously.
steps.reasoning_content:
This is a complex question that requires me to:

1. Find SpaceX's Connectivity segment operating income and Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025 from the S-1 filing
2. Calculate the implied depreciation and amortization add-back (the gap between EBITDA and operating income)
3. Find Viasat's most recent 10-K and get satellite depreciation as a percentage of satellite-segment revenue
4. Compare directionally and analyze the structural differences between LEO and GEO constellation economics

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and searching for Viasat's 10-K.

arguments.prompt: What is the Connectivity segment revenue for FY2025 (fiscal year ended December 31, 2025) from {{spacex_s1}}? Also retrieve any information about satellite useful life assumptions, depreciation policies, or satellite constellation details.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial documents, here is the information regarding the Connectivity segment's revenue for FY2025, as well as the details on satellite useful life assumptions and depreciation policies:

### **Connectivity Segment Revenue (FY2025)**
* **Total Revenue:** **$11,387 million** (for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025). 
*(Note: This represents a significant increase from $7,599 million in 2024, driven by the growth of the Starlink Subscriber base, enterprise, and government sales).*

---

### **Satellite Useful Life Assumptions & Depreciation Policies**
**1. Useful Life Estimation Criteria:**
The company estimates the useful lives of its satellite assets based on several factors, including:
* Engineering studies and historical on-orbit performance
* Propellant life and utilization patterns
* Design enhancements across generations
* Planned transitions to newer satellite technology
* *Management periodically reviews these estimates with engineering and operations teams and revises them as additional data becomes available.*

**2. Specific Satellite Lifespans:**
* **Broadband Satellites:** Estimated to have a **5-year** useful life.
* **First-Generation Mobile Satellites:** Estimated to have a **3-year** useful life.

**3. Depreciation Policies:**
* **Method:** Satellite assets are depreciated using the **straight-line method** over their estimated useful lives. *(Note: Flight vehicles/rockets are an exception and are depreciated based on the expected number of average flights per vehicle).*
* **Capitalization vs. Expensing:** Expenditures for standard maintenance and repairs that do not extend the life of the assets are expensed as incurred. However, significant refurbishments, renewals, and enhancements that increase an asset's functionality, output, or expected life are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.
* **Cost of Revenue:** The Connectivity segment’s cost of revenue explicitly includes depreciation (which encompasses launch, satellite, and ground infrastructure costs). In 2025, this depreciation expense grew significantly due to the increased number of satellites placed into orbit and the higher cadence of Starlink flights. 

### **Satellite Constellation Details**
* The constellation includes both **broadband satellites** (consumer/enterprise internet) and **mobile satellites** (direct-to-cell/mobile connectivity).
* The company experienced a massive expansion of its constellation in 2025, noted by a surge in capitalized launch and satellite costs, an increase in Starlink flights (from 89 launches in 2024 to 122 launches in 2025), and the ongoing transition to next-generation satellite production.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial disclosures, Viasat does not report a standalone "Satellite" segment. Instead, its satellite operations, bandwidth, and related services are housed within the **Communication Services** segment. Furthermore, the company does not break out "satellite-only" depreciation expense on the income statement; rather, it reports aggregate depreciation for the segment (which includes satellites, earth stations, networking equipment, and CPE units). 

Here are the metrics for the **Communication Services** segment for the most recent fiscal year (**FY25**, ended March 31, 2025):

### 1. Requested Financial Metrics (FY25)

*   **Segment Revenue:** $3,298,485 thousand ($3.30 billion)
*   **Segment Operating Income (Loss):** $(50,222) thousand (-$50.2 million operating loss)

*(For additional context on asset depreciation, the notes disclose that the accumulated depreciation specifically for CPE units included in "property, equipment and satellites, net" was $328.0 million as of March 31, 2025).*

---

Based on the provided text, here are Viasat's satellite useful life assumptions, depreciation policies, and the disclosures regarding depreciation and related expenses:

### 1. Satellite Useful Life Assumptions
*   **Estimation Factors:** At the time satellites are placed in commercial service, the useful life is estimated based on an analysis of the satellite’s performance against the original manufacturer’s orbital design life, estimated fuel levels and consumption rates, and historical operating trends.
*   **Periodic Review:** The company periodically reviews the remaining estimated useful lives to determine if revisions are necessary.
*   **Specific Useful Life Ranges:** 
    *   **Satellites:** 7 to 17 years
    *   **Satellite Ka-band capacity obtained under finance leases:** 7 to 15 years
*   **Risk Factors:** The company acknowledges that actual operational life may be shorter than the estimated "mission life" due to factors such as component durability, orbit maintenance, fuel consumption, solar panel degradation, space environment anomalies, or technological obsolescence.

### 2. Depreciation Policies for Satellites
*   **Initial Recording:** Satellites are recorded at cost (or fair value at the acquisition date for certain acquired satellites), net of accumulated depreciation.
*   **Capitalized Costs:** Capitalized satellite costs include construction and launch costs, launch and in-orbit testing insurance, the net present value of performance incentives payable to manufacturers, direct monitoring and support costs, and interest costs incurred during construction.
*   **Subsequent Costs:** Costs for additions, major renewals, and betterments are capitalized and depreciated over the asset's remaining life. Maintenance, repairs, and minor renewals are expensed as incurred.
*   **Depreciation Method:** Satellites (along with earth stations, networking equipment, CPE units, and installation costs) are depreciated over their estimated useful lives.
*   **Disposals:** When assets are sold or disposed of, the cost and accumulated depreciation are removed from the accounts, and any resulting gain or loss is recognized in operations.

### 3. Satellite Depreciation Expense Disclosed
**The specific dollar amount for periodic satellite depreciation expense is not disclosed in the provided text chunks.** However, the text does disclose the following related financial figures regarding satellite assets, impairments, and customer equipment:
*   **Satellite Impairment Charges (FY24):** Due to anomalies with the ViaSat-3 F1 and I-6 F2 satellites and Inmarsat integration impacts, the company recorded a **$1.67 billion** reduction to the carrying value of satellites under construction. This was partially offset by **$770.0 million** in insurance claim receivables, resulting in a **net loss of approximately $905.5 million** recorded in Selling, General, and Administrative (SG&A) expenses. 
*   **Insurance Recoveries:** The company received **$251.5 million** (FY25) and **$508.6 million** (FY24) in insurance recovery proceeds related to these satellite anomaly claims, with an additional $10.0 million received subsequent to FY25.
*   **CPE Unit Accumulated Depreciation:** While not the satellites themselves, the accumulated depreciation for Customer Premise Equipment (CPE) units leased to customers was disclosed as **$328.0 million** (against $526.0 million in total cost) as of March 31, 2025, and **$267.4 million** (against $567.5 million in total cost) as of March 31, 2024. 
*   **MD&A Context:** Management notes that placing a new satellite into commercial service triggers a cycle of increased operating costs that explicitly includes depreciation, though the exact dollar impact of this depreciation is not quantified in the provided excerpts.

Based on the provided financial disclosures, here is the breakdown of Viasat's **Property, Equipment, and Satellites, net**, along with the specific details regarding satellites and your request for non-GAAP measures.

### 1. Property, Equipment, and Satellites Breakdown
The following is the gross cost breakdown by asset category as of March 31, 2025, and March 31, 2024 *(all figures in thousands)*:

| Asset Category | As of March 31, 2025 | As of March 31, 2024 |

| Leasehold improvements (2-20 years) | $313,249 | $209,162 |
| Buildings (12-38 years) | $15,388 | $16,647 |
| Land | $19,661 | $20,787 |
| Construction in progress | $722,194 | $1,301,376 |
| **Satellites (7-17 years)** | **$3,405,067** | **$3,324,458** |
| **Satellite Ka-band capacity (finance leases, 7-15 years)**| **$338,201** | **$177,576** |
| **Satellites under construction** | **$2,205,305** | **$1,976,469** |
| **Total Gross Property, Equipment & Satellites** | **$11,440,273** | **$10,651,781** |
| *Less: Accumulated depreciation and amortization* | *$(4,034,609)* | *$(3,094,575)* |
| **Total Property, Equipment & Satellites, net** | **$7,405,664** | **$7,557,206** |

### 2. Satellite-Specific Gross Cost and Accumulated Depreciation
**Gross Cost for Satellites:**
The gross cost specifically tied to satellite assets (combining in-orbit satellites, leased Ka-band capacity, and satellites under construction) is:
*   **Fiscal 2025:** $5,948,573 (approx. $5.95 billion)
*   **Fiscal 2024:** $5,478,503 (approx. $5.48 billion)

**Accumulated Depreciation for Satellites:**
The provided text **does not isolate the accumulated depreciation exclusively for satellites**. The accumulated depreciation and amortization of **$4,034,609** (2025) and **$3,094,575** (2024) is an *aggregate* figure that applies to the entire "Property, equipment and satellites" balance. 
*(Note: The only specific sub-category accumulated depreciation explicitly broken out in the notes is for Customer Premise Equipment (CPE) units, which had $328.0 million in accumulated depreciation in 2025 and $267.4 million in 2024).*

### 3. Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation & Non-GAAP Measures
**Not Available in Provided Text:** 
The provided excerpts from the 10-K filing (which include the Consolidated Balance Sheets, Statements of Operations, Common-Size Income Statements, Quarterly Financial Data, and Accounting Policies) **do not contain** any Adjusted EBITDA reconciliations or Non-GAAP financial measures. To find Viasat's Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation, you would typically need to refer to the "Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations" (MD&A) section or the company's earnings press releases, which are not included in these specific text chunks.

Based on the provided prospectus excerpts, here are the details regarding the SpaceX Connectivity segment (Starlink), broken down by your requested categories:

### 1. Depreciation and Amortization Details (Connectivity Segment)
The Connectivity segment has seen significant increases in depreciation and amortization, which the MD&A explicitly attributes to the growing number of satellites placed into orbit. 
* **Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026):** $783 million
* **Q1 2025 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2025):** $510 million
* **Full Year 2024:** $1,508 million
* **Full Year 2023:** $884 million
* **MD&A Context:** The company noted that Cost of Revenue for Q1 2026 increased by $437 million in the Connectivity segment, which was primarily "driven by an increase in depreciation related to the number of satellites placed into orbit."

### 2. Satellite Constellation Size & Satellites in Orbit
As of **March 31, 2026**, SpaceX's Connectivity segment operates a massive and dominant low-Earth orbit (LEO) constellation:
* **Total Starlink Satellites in Orbit:** Approximately **9,600** broadband and mobile satellites. 
* **Market Dominance:** These 9,600 satellites account for approximately **75% of all active maneuverable satellites** in orbit globally (out of an industry total of ~12,700).
* **Constellation Composition:** The majority of the broadband constellation is composed of second-generation **V2 Mini** satellites.
* **Mobile Constellation:** The dedicated satellite-to-mobile constellation has grown from approximately 360 "V1 Mobile" satellites in January 2025 to approximately **650 mobile V1 Mobile satellites** as of March 31, 2026, serving ~7.4 million monthly unique devices across ~30 countries.

### 3. Capital Expenditure (Connectivity Segment)
Capital expenditures for the Connectivity segment have scaled steadily to support constellation expansion, ground infrastructure, and spectrum acquisitions:
* **Q1 2026:** $1,332 million
* **Q1 2025:** $814 million
* **Full Year 2025:** $4,178 million
* **Full Year 2024:** $3,498 million
* **Full Year 2023:** $2,455 million

### 4. Satellite Replenishment, Deployment Rates, and Future Capacity
The text highlights an aggressive roadmap for satellite replenishment, next-generation deployments, and capacity expansion:
* **Next-Generation V3 Satellites & Starship Deployment:** SpaceX expects to commence deploying its V3 satellites in the **second half of 2026** using the Starship launch vehicle. 
  * **Capacity:** Each V3 satellite is designed to offer **1 Tbps** of downlink capacity.
  * **Deployment Rate/Cadence:** A single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to **60 V3 satellites** to LEO. This represents a potential **twenty-fold increase** in Starlink downlink capacity deployed per launch compared to a Falcon 9 launch.
* **2025 Replenishment & Upgrades:** Throughout 2025, the company focused on launching and operating higher-throughput satellites to increase available network capacity and improve service consistency during peak usage periods.
* **Regulatory Authorizations & Spectrum Expansion:** The company noted its authorization to deploy **7,500 satellites** (including operations in the 2GHz spectrum band). This is tied to a massive $19.6 billion equity and cash agreement to acquire 65 MHz of U.S. spectrum and global Mobile Satellite Service spectrum licenses from EchoStar (expected to close in November 2027). 
* **Strategic Impact:** The company explicitly states that "investments in satellite scale, per-satellite capacity, and expanded capabilities are instrumental to the growth and operating performance of our Connectivity segment, enabling us to onboard new users while improving service."

results.content:
Based on the provided text, there is **no mention** of Adjusted EBITDA, EBITDA, or any other non-GAAP profitability measures for the Communication Services segment. 

Additionally, the provided text **does not contain** a discussion comparing the impact of satellite depreciation on operating income versus EBITDA. 

However, the text does detail the cyclical financial impacts of satellite launches and depreciation on the company's **GAAP operating profit** and general financial results. Here is what the provided text outlines regarding those impacts:

*   **Cyclical Operating Costs & Depreciation:** In connection with the launch and commercialization of new satellites (such as ViaSat-2 historically, and the ViaSat-3 constellation currently), Viasat incurs a cycle of increased operating costs that negatively impact financial results during the ramp-up period. These increased costs explicitly include **depreciation**, amortization of capitalized software development, earth station connectivity, marketing, logistics, and customer care.
*   **Interest Expense Impacts:** Financial results are further pressured because the company can no longer capitalize interest expense relating to the debt incurred for satellite construction and gateway equipment once the satellite enters commercial service.
*   **Impact on Operating Profit:** The text warns that there is "no assurance that we will be successful in significantly increasing revenues or achieving or maintaining **operating profit** in our communication services segment" due to these heavy upfront investments and global business offsets. 
*   **Capacity Constraints:** The company experiences constrained bandwidth supply and capacity constraints on existing satellites in the lead-up to new launches (noting constraints experienced since fiscal year 2023 pending the ViaSat-3 launch).
*   **Long-Term Segment Earnings:** Eventually, as services on the new satellites scale, the broadband revenue base expands and operating cost efficiencies are gained, which together are expected to yield "incremental segment earnings contributions." 

*(Note: The provided financial tables only report GAAP "Segment operating profit (loss)", which for the Communication Services segment was $(50.2) million for the fiscal year ended March 31, 2025, and $(817.1) million for the fiscal year ended March 31, 2024, heavily impacted by satellite and ground network impairments rather than just standard depreciation.)*

However, the question is about the structural difference. Let me think about this more carefully:

But wait - SpaceX's D&A as % of revenue is actually LOWER than Viasat's. This is because SpaceX has achieved massive revenue scale. The question is about the structural nature of the D&A burden.

The structural difference is:

- **LEO model**: Thousands of satellites, short lives (5 years), lower per-satellite cost, continuous capex treadmill, depreciation is a constant and growing charge

The EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion is particularly important for LEO operators because:
1. The D&A charge is structurally larger in absolute terms due to constellation scale
2. Shorter useful lives mean faster depreciation per dollar of capex
3. Continuous replenishment means D&A never "runs off" - it's a permanent, growing charge
4. EBITDA can significantly overstate the cash-generating ability because it ignores the mandatory capex needed to maintain the constellation
5. For GEO operators, satellites eventually become fully depreciated while still generating revenue; for LEO operators, the constellation must be continuously replaced

2. LEO has many more satellites (9,600 vs ~10-15 for GEO) → larger absolute depreciation base
3. LEO requires continuous replenishment → D&A is a permanent, non-declining charge
4. GEO satellites can operate past their depreciation period → "free" revenue after depreciation runs off
5. EBITDA overstates cash generation for LEO because it ignores the mandatory maintenance capex
6. The EBITDA-to-OpIncome gap is the key metric for understanding true profitability

arguments.final_result:
## SpaceX Connectivity vs. Viasat Communication Services: Depreciation, EBITDA Conversion, and LEO vs. GEO Structural Economics

---

### 1. SpaceX Connectivity Segment — Implied D&A Add-Back (FY2025)

From the SpaceX S-1 reconciliation table for the year ended December 31, 2025:

| Metric | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Revenue | $11,387 |
| Income from Operations | $4,423 |
| **Add: Depreciation & Amortization** | **$2,376** |
| Add: Share-Based Compensation | $369 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

**The implied D&A add-back embedded in the EBITDA bridge is $2,376 million.**

The total gap between Segment Adjusted EBITDA and Operating Income is **$2,745 million** ($7,168 − $4,423), of which D&A constitutes **86.6%** ($2,376 / $2,745) and SBC constitutes the remaining 13.4%.

Key ratios for SpaceX Connectivity:
- **D&A as % of Revenue:** 20.87% ($2,376 / $11,387)
- **D&A as % of Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** 33.15% ($2,376 / $7,168)
- **Operating Margin:** 38.84%
- **EBITDA Margin:** 62.95%
- **Capex / D&A Ratio:** 1.76× ($4,178M capex / $2,376M D&A) — indicating the constellation is still net-growing

---

### 2. Viasat Communication Services Segment — Depreciation Burden (FY2025, ended March 31, 2025)

From Viasat's 10-K filed May 27, 2025:

| Metric | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| Communication Services Revenue | $3,298.5 |
| Segment Depreciation | $988.4 |
| Segment Amortization (ex-acquired intangibles) | $41.4 |
| **Total D&A** | **$1,029.8** |
| Stock-Based Compensation | $48.8 |
| **Segment Operating Income (Loss)** | **$(50.2)** |
| Implied EBITDA Proxy (OpIncome + D&A + SBC) | ~$1,028.4 |

Key ratios for Viasat Communication Services:
- **D&A as % of Revenue:** 31.22% ($1,029.8 / $3,298.5)
- **Depreciation alone as % of Revenue:** 29.97%
- **Operating Margin:** −1.52%
- **Implied EBITDA Margin:** ~31.18%
- **Satellite useful life assumptions:** 7–17 years (GEO satellites); Ka-band finance leases: 7–15 years

---

### 3. Directional Comparison

| Metric | SpaceX Connectivity (LEO) | Viasat Comm. Services (GEO) |
|---|---|---|
| D&A as % of Revenue | **20.87%** | **31.22%** |
| D&A as % of EBITDA | **33.15%** | **~100.1%** |
| Operating Margin | **+38.84%** | **−1.52%** |
| EBITDA Margin | 62.95% | ~31.18% |
| Satellite Useful Life | 5 yrs (broadband), 3 yrs (mobile) | 7–17 yrs |
| Satellites in Orbit | ~9,600 | ~10–15 (GEO) |

**Critical observation:** Viasat's D&A consumes essentially **100% of its EBITDA**, converting a ~$1.0 billion positive EBITDA proxy into a **$(50) million operating loss**. SpaceX's D&A consumes only **33% of EBITDA**, leaving $4.4 billion in operating income. On the surface, this makes SpaceX's EBITDA-to-OpIncome conversion appear far more favorable.

However, this comparison is heavily influenced by **revenue scale** ($11.4B vs. $3.3B) and **constellation maturity**. The structural dynamics are more nuanced than the headline percentages suggest.

---

### 4. What This Reveals About LEO vs. GEO Structural Economics

#### A. The Depreciation Velocity Paradox

LEO satellites have **dramatically shorter useful lives** (5 years for SpaceX broadband, 3 years for first-gen mobile) compared to GEO satellites (7–17 years for Viasat). On a per-satellite basis, this means:

- A $500K–$1M LEO satellite depreciates at **$100K–$200K/year** over 5 years
- A $500M–$1B GEO satellite depreciates at **$33M–$143M/year** over 7–17 years

While each individual LEO satellite carries far less depreciation, the **sheer volume** (~9,600 satellites) creates an aggregate D&A charge ($2.376B) that is **2.3× Viasat's total segment D&A** ($1.030B) — despite SpaceX's satellites being individually orders of magnitude cheaper.

#### B. The Continuous Replenishment Treadmill

This is the most structurally important difference:

- **GEO model:** A Viasat satellite launched in 2015 with a 15-year life will be fully depreciated by 2030 but may continue generating revenue through 2035+. During those "post-depreciation" years, the satellite produces revenue with **zero depreciation expense** — a period of highly accretive cash flow. GEO operators experience **lumpy capex cycles** followed by **harvest periods**.

- **LEO model:** SpaceX's 5-year useful life means satellites placed in orbit in 2023 will be fully depreciated by 2028 — but they will also likely be **deorbited or non-functional** by then. There is **no harvest period**. The constellation must be continuously replenished, meaning D&A is a **permanent, non-declining, and growing** charge. SpaceX's capex-to-D&A ratio of **1.76×** in FY2025 confirms the constellation is still expanding, and D&A will continue to grow.

#### C. Why EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Conversion Is Critical for LEO Operators

The comparison reveals three reasons this conversion is analytically essential when evaluating LEO broadband operators as a class:

**1. EBITDA structurally overstates sustainable cash generation for LEO operators.**
For GEO operators, EBITDA is a reasonable proxy for cash flow during the "harvest" phase of a satellite's life, because maintenance capex is minimal. For LEO operators, EBITDA ignores the **mandatory, continuous replenishment capex** required simply to maintain the constellation at current service levels. SpaceX's $4.178B in Connectivity capex vs. $2.376B in D&A means the business must spend **$1.76 in capex for every $1 of depreciation** just to grow — and even maintaining the constellation requires capex roughly equal to D&A on a steady-state basis.

**2. The D&A charge is a better proxy for long-run maintenance capex in LEO than in GEO.**
In GEO, D&A often significantly exceeds actual maintenance capex during harvest years (the satellite is depreciating but doesn't need replacement). In LEO, D&A **understates** the true economic cost because each satellite must be physically replaced. The $2.376B D&A charge in SpaceX's Connectivity segment is not a "non-cash add-back" that can be ignored — it represents real satellites that will need to be replaced with real launch costs within 5 years.

**3. Scale is the only path to EBITDA-to-OpIncome convergence in LEO.**
SpaceX's 38.84% operating margin demonstrates that LEO economics *can* work — but only at massive revenue scale ($11.4B) that dilutes the D&A burden to 20.87% of revenue. Viasat's near-zero operating income at $3.3B in revenue shows that satellite-dependent businesses with high D&A loads cannot achieve operating profitability without either (a) sufficient revenue scale or (b) long-lived assets that create harvest periods. LEO operators lack option (b), making option (a) the only path — and making the EBITDA-to-OpIncome gap the single most important metric for assessing whether a LEO operator has achieved sufficient scale to overcome its structural depreciation treadmill.

---

### Summary

SpaceX's Connectivity segment embeds **$2,376 million in D&A** within its FY2025 EBITDA bridge — a charge that represents 20.87% of revenue and 33.15% of Segment Adjusted EBITDA. Viasat's Communication Services segment carries D&A of **$1,029.8 million**, representing 31.22% of revenue and approximately **100% of its implied EBITDA**, converting positive EBITDA into a $(50) million operating loss.

The directional comparison reveals that while LEO constellations benefit from lower per-satellite costs and massive revenue scale, they face a structurally permanent D&A burden driven by shorter useful lives (5 vs. 7–17 years), higher satellite counts (~9,600 vs. ~10–15), and continuous replenishment requirements. Unlike GEO operators, LEO operators have no "post-depreciation harvest period" — making EBITDA a potentially misleading measure of value creation and rendering the EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion the critical analytical lens for evaluating LEO broadband operators as a class.

---

    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/797721/000095017025077138/vsat-20250331.htm",
      "name": "Viasat Inc. 10-K Annual Report (FY2025, ended March 31, 2025; filed May 27, 2025)"
    }
  ]

results.content:
## SpaceX Connectivity vs. Viasat Communication Services: Depreciation, EBITDA Conversion, and LEO vs. GEO Structural Economics

---

### 1. SpaceX Connectivity Segment — Implied D&A Add-Back (FY2025)

From the SpaceX S-1 reconciliation table for the year ended December 31, 2025:

| Metric | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Revenue | $11,387 |
| Income from Operations | $4,423 |
| **Add: Depreciation & Amortization** | **$2,376** |
| Add: Share-Based Compensation | $369 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

**The implied D&A add-back embedded in the EBITDA bridge is $2,376 million.**

The total gap between Segment Adjusted EBITDA and Operating Income is **$2,745 million** ($7,168 − $4,423), of which D&A constitutes **86.6%** ($2,376 / $2,745) and SBC constitutes the remaining 13.4%.

Key ratios for SpaceX Connectivity:
- **D&A as % of Revenue:** 20.87% ($2,376 / $11,387)
- **D&A as % of Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** 33.15% ($2,376 / $7,168)
- **Operating Margin:** 38.84%
- **EBITDA Margin:** 62.95%
- **Capex / D&A Ratio:** 1.76× ($4,178M capex / $2,376M D&A) — indicating the constellation is still net-growing

---

### 2. Viasat Communication Services Segment — Depreciation Burden (FY2025, ended March 31, 2025)

From Viasat's 10-K filed May 27, 2025:

| Metric | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| Communication Services Revenue | $3,298.5 |
| Segment Depreciation | $988.4 |
| Segment Amortization (ex-acquired intangibles) | $41.4 |
| **Total D&A** | **$1,029.8** |
| Stock-Based Compensation | $48.8 |
| **Segment Operating Income (Loss)** | **$(50.2)** |
| Implied EBITDA Proxy (OpIncome + D&A + SBC) | ~$1,028.4 |

Key ratios for Viasat Communication Services:
- **D&A as % of Revenue:** 31.22% ($1,029.8 / $3,298.5)
- **Depreciation alone as % of Revenue:** 29.97%
- **Operating Margin:** −1.52%
- **Implied EBITDA Margin:** ~31.18%
- **Satellite useful life assumptions:** 7–17 years (GEO satellites); Ka-band finance leases: 7–15 years

---

### 3. Directional Comparison

| Metric | SpaceX Connectivity (LEO) | Viasat Comm. Services (GEO) |
|---|---|---|
| D&A as % of Revenue | **20.87%** | **31.22%** |
| D&A as % of EBITDA | **33.15%** | **~100.1%** |
| Operating Margin | **+38.84%** | **−1.52%** |
| EBITDA Margin | 62.95% | ~31.18% |
| Satellite Useful Life | 5 yrs (broadband), 3 yrs (mobile) | 7–17 yrs |
| Satellites in Orbit | ~9,600 | ~10–15 (GEO) |

**Critical observation:** Viasat's D&A consumes essentially **100% of its EBITDA**, converting a ~$1.0 billion positive EBITDA proxy into a **$(50) million operating loss**. SpaceX's D&A consumes only **33% of EBITDA**, leaving $4.4 billion in operating income. On the surface, this makes SpaceX's EBITDA-to-OpIncome conversion appear far more favorable.

However, this comparison is heavily influenced by **revenue scale** ($11.4B vs. $3.3B) and **constellation maturity**. The structural dynamics are more nuanced than the headline percentages suggest.

---

### 4. What This Reveals About LEO vs. GEO Structural Economics

#### A. The Depreciation Velocity Paradox

LEO satellites have **dramatically shorter useful lives** (5 years for SpaceX broadband, 3 years for first-gen mobile) compared to GEO satellites (7–17 years for Viasat). On a per-satellite basis, this means:

- A $500K–$1M LEO satellite depreciates at **$100K–$200K/year** over 5 years
- A $500M–$1B GEO satellite depreciates at **$33M–$143M/year** over 7–17 years

While each individual LEO satellite carries far less depreciation, the **sheer volume** (~9,600 satellites) creates an aggregate D&A charge ($2.376B) that is **2.3× Viasat's total segment D&A** ($1.030B) — despite SpaceX's satellites being individually orders of magnitude cheaper.

#### B. The Continuous Replenishment Treadmill

This is the most structurally important difference:

- **GEO model:** A Viasat satellite launched in 2015 with a 15-year life will be fully depreciated by 2030 but may continue generating revenue through 2035+. During those "post-depreciation" years, the satellite produces revenue with **zero depreciation expense** — a period of highly accretive cash flow. GEO operators experience **lumpy capex cycles** followed by **harvest periods**.

- **LEO model:** SpaceX's 5-year useful life means satellites placed in orbit in 2023 will be fully depreciated by 2028 — but they will also likely be **deorbited or non-functional** by then. There is **no harvest period**. The constellation must be continuously replenished, meaning D&A is a **permanent, non-declining, and growing** charge. SpaceX's capex-to-D&A ratio of **1.76×** in FY2025 confirms the constellation is still expanding, and D&A will continue to grow.

#### C. Why EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Conversion Is Critical for LEO Operators

The comparison reveals three reasons this conversion is analytically essential when evaluating LEO broadband operators as a class:

**1. EBITDA structurally overstates sustainable cash generation for LEO operators.**
For GEO operators, EBITDA is a reasonable proxy for cash flow during the "harvest" phase of a satellite's life, because maintenance capex is minimal. For LEO operators, EBITDA ignores the **mandatory, continuous replenishment capex** required simply to maintain the constellation at current service levels. SpaceX's $4.178B in Connectivity capex vs. $2.376B in D&A means the business must spend **$1.76 in capex for every $1 of depreciation** just to grow — and even maintaining the constellation requires capex roughly equal to D&A on a steady-state basis.

**2. The D&A charge is a better proxy for long-run maintenance capex in LEO than in GEO.**
In GEO, D&A often significantly exceeds actual maintenance capex during harvest years (the satellite is depreciating but doesn't need replacement). In LEO, D&A **understates** the true economic cost because each satellite must be physically replaced. The $2.376B D&A charge in SpaceX's Connectivity segment is not a "non-cash add-back" that can be ignored — it represents real satellites that will need to be replaced with real launch costs within 5 years.

**3. Scale is the only path to EBITDA-to-OpIncome convergence in LEO.**
SpaceX's 38.84% operating margin demonstrates that LEO economics *can* work — but only at massive revenue scale ($11.4B) that dilutes the D&A burden to 20.87% of revenue. Viasat's near-zero operating income at $3.3B in revenue shows that satellite-dependent businesses with high D&A loads cannot achieve operating profitability without either (a) sufficient revenue scale or (b) long-lived assets that create harvest periods. LEO operators lack option (b), making option (a) the only path — and making the EBITDA-to-OpIncome gap the single most important metric for assessing whether a LEO operator has achieved sufficient scale to overcome its structural depreciation treadmill.

---

### Summary

SpaceX's Connectivity segment embeds **$2,376 million in D&A** within its FY2025 EBITDA bridge — a charge that represents 20.87% of revenue and 33.15% of Segment Adjusted EBITDA. Viasat's Communication Services segment carries D&A of **$1,029.8 million**, representing 31.22% of revenue and approximately **100% of its implied EBITDA**, converting positive EBITDA into a $(50) million operating loss.

The directional comparison reveals that while LEO constellations benefit from lower per-satellite costs and massive revenue scale, they face a structurally permanent D&A burden driven by shorter useful lives (5 vs. 7–17 years), higher satellite counts (~9,600 vs. ~10–15), and continuous replenishment requirements. Unlike GEO operators, LEO operators have no "post-depreciation harvest period" — making EBITDA a potentially misleading measure of value creation and rendering the EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion the critical analytical lens for evaluating LEO broadband operators as a class.
